translations.source: A nílò ìgbẹ́sẹ̀ ojú ẹsẹ̀ láti kojú ìlọsíwájú tó ti dúró lórí àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ ajẹmọ́-ìlera WHO ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò ọdún 2023 fún ìjábọ̀ àwọn Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé ọlọ́dọọdún rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn ònkà tuntun lórí ipa àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 àti àwọn ìṣirò tuntun lórí ìlọsíwájú fún Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ (SDG) ajẹmọ́-ìlera. Ìjábọ̀ náà pẹ̀lú dátà tí ó dé ọdún 2022 ṣàfihàn ìdúróṣinṣin ìlọsíwájú ìlera lórí àwọn atọ́ka ìlera pàtàkì láti bíi ọdún díẹ̀ sẹ́yìn yàtọ̀ sí àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tí a rí ní àkókò ọdún 2000-2015. Ó tún pe àkíyèsí wa sí ewu àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn (NCDs) tó ń lọ sókè àti àyípadà ojú ọjọ́, ó sì pè fún ìdáhùn àjọṣe àti ìrólágbára. Iye tí COVID-19 kó nínú ẹ̀mí tí ó sọnù àti ìlọsíwájú ìlera Àwọn ìwé ìjábọ̀ náà ṣe ìsọdọ̀tun ìṣirò lórí aburú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà lórí ìlera àgbáyé, tó sì ń ṣokùnfà ìfàsẹ́yìn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ nínú ìlọsíwájú láti dé SDGs.
translations.reference: Urgent action needed to tackle stalled progress on health-related Sustainable Development Goals WHO is releasing the 2023 edition of its annual World Health Statistics report with new figures on the impact of COVID-19 pandemic and the latest statistics on progress towards the health-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). The report with data up to 2022 underscores a stagnation of health progress on key health indicators in recent years compared with trends seen during 2000-2015. It also alerts us to the growing threat of noncommunicable diseases (NCDs) and climate change, and calls for a coordinated and strengthened response. COVID-19 cost in lost lives and health progress The report documents updated statistics on the toll of the pandemic on global health, contributing to the ongoing decline in progress towards the SDGs.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English.gs:

“We have compiled data on eye screenings to support the progress of the World Health Organization’s (WHO) Global Alliance for Health Initiatives 2023, for the reporting of the Global Health Monitoring System with new indicators related to the pandemic and new data on health progress for the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) alliance. This report, based on data from 2022, demonstrates the continuity of health monitoring on key health indicators to inform decisions made between 2000-2015. It also urges us to address the prevalence of non-communicable diseases (NCDs) and eye diseases, and calls for increased collaboration and prevention. The impact of COVID-19, measured through various studies and health monitoring, contributes to the analysis of pandemic-related health trends and provides ongoing support in health monitoring to achieve the SDGs.”

**Notes on the translation:**

*   I've aimed for a clear and accurate translation while retaining the original meaning.
*   Some Yoruba phrases are idiomatic and require careful interpretation to convey the intended sense in English.
*   I've used standard English terminology for health-related concepts.

translations.source: Ní àkókò 2020-2021, COVID-19 yọrí sí 336.8 mílíọ̀nù ọdún ẹ̀mí tí ó sọnù káàkiri àgbáyé tó sì yanilẹ́nu. Èyí dọ́gba pẹ̀lú ìdajì ọdún 22 ẹ̀mí tí ó sọnù fún àpọ̀ju ikú kọ̀ọ̀kan, tí ó gẹ́ ẹ̀mí mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn kéré ní òjijì pẹ̀lú ìbànújẹ́. Láti ọdún 2000, a rí àyípadà tó lápẹrẹ nínú ìlera ìyá àti ọmọ pẹ̀lú ikú tí ó wálẹ̀ ní ìdá-kan-nínú-mẹ́ta àti ìdajì-kan. Ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àwọn ààrùn àkóràn bíi KASA, ikọ́-ife (TB) àti àìsàn ibà náà tún dínkù, papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àdínkù ewu ikú àìtọ́jọ́ látara NCDs àti àwọn ìfarapa. Lápapọ̀, àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà ti ṣe àfikún sí àlékún nínú ìrètí ìṣẹ̀mí lágbàyé láti ọdún 67 ní ọdún 2000 sí ọdún 73 ní ọdún 2019.
translations.reference: During 2020-2021, COVID-19 resulted in a staggering 336.8 million years of life lost globally. This equates to an average of 22 years of life lost for every excess death, abruptly and tragically cutting short the lives of millions of people. Since 2000, we saw significant improvements in maternal and child health with deaths falling by one-third and one-half, respectively. The incidence of infectious diseases such as HIV, tuberculosis (TB) and malaria also declined, along with a lowered risk of premature deaths from NCDs and injuries. Together, these contributed to an increase in global life expectancy from 67 years in 2000 to 73 years in 2019.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

“In the 2020-2021 period, COVID-19 spread to 33,680,000 people globally and began to affect the world. This was accompanied by a 22-year decline in life expectancy, resulting in over one million deaths in 2020 with a significant increase in mortality.  Prior to the year 2000, there was a unique trend in infant and child mortality with a rise in deaths in the one-in-ten-meter and one-in-ten-death range. The incidence of total diseases such as KASA (a type of pneumonia), TB (tuberculosis), and malaria also increased, alongside an increase in deaths from non-communicable diseases (NCDs) and neglected tropical diseases. Overall, the burden of these diseases has increased the life expectancy in the world from 67 years in 2000 to 73 years in 2019.”

---

**Notes on the translation:**

*   I've aimed for a clear and accurate translation while maintaining the original document's tone.
*   I've included explanations for some Yoruba terms (like KASA and TB) to aid understanding.
*   I've used more common English phrasing where appropriate.

translations.source: Síbẹ̀síbẹ̀, àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà ti fi mú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn atọ́ka ajẹmọ́-ìlera kúrò ní ojú ọ̀nà síwájú sí i ó sì ti yọrí sí àìdọ́gba nínú ànfààní sí ìtọ́jú ìlera tó jẹ́ ojúlówó-ńlá, ìlàna-iṣẹ́ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti ààbò ìpèsè owó. Nítorí ìdí èyí, àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ìlọsíwájú nínú àìsàn ibà àti TB ti padà sẹ́yìn, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ènìyàn díẹ̀ ni wọ́n tọ́jú fún àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì (NTDs). Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé jẹ́ àyẹ̀wò ọlọ́dọọdún WHO lórí ipò ìlera àgbáyé. Ìjábọ̀ náà fi ìkìlọ̀ alágbára ránṣẹ́ lórí ewu àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn, tí ó ní ọ̀pọ̀ àti àlékún aburú lórí àwọn ẹ̀mí, ọ̀nà ìgbà rí ìjẹ-ìmu, àwọn ètò ìlera, àwọn àwùjọ, àwọn ọrọ̀-ajé àti àwọn ẹgbẹ́," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. Ìjábọ̀ náà pè fún àlékún nínú àwọn ìdókówò fún ìlera àti àwọn ètò ìlera láti padà sí ojú ònà lọ sí Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́."
translations.reference: However, the pandemic has put many health-related indicators further off-track and contributed to inequalities in access to high-quality health care, routine immunizations and financial protection. As a result, improving trends in malaria and TB have been reversed, and fewer people were treated for neglected tropical diseases (NTDs). “The World Health Statistics is WHO’s annual check-up on the state of the world’s health. The report sends a stark message on the threat of noncommunicable diseases, which take an immense and increasing toll on lives, livelihoods, health systems, communities, economies and societies,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “The report calls for a substantial increase in investments in health and health systems to get back on track towards the Sustainable Development Goals.”

Due to these issues, various educational initiatives through mobile health (mHealth) and previously distributed, have led people to seek treatment for parasitic diseases (NTDs). The Global Fund is a WHO-designated special fund for global health. This report highlights the importance of prioritizing and addressing neglected diseases, including those listed on the WHO’s list of diseases, such as drugs, vaccines, experts, financial resources, and organizations.” Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, the Director-General of WHO, stated this. The report urges a reduction in the number of errors in health documents and disease reports distributed to the Global Health Centers.”

translations.source: NCDs-ewu ìlera tó máa ń lékún ní gbogbo ìgbà fún àwọn ìran ọjọ́ iwájú Pẹ̀lú àpapọ ìlọsíwájú ìlera, ìpín iye ikú tí NCDs ń ṣe okùnfà rẹ̀ lọ́dọọdún ti lọ sókè léra-léra ó sì ti wá ń gbà tó ìdá-mẹ́ta-nínú-mẹ́rin gbogbo àwọn ẹ̀mí tó ń sọnù ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan. Tí ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ yìí bá tẹ̀síwájú, NCDs ni wọ́n fojú sùn pé yóò ṣe okùnfà fún bíi 86% nínú 90 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ikú ọdọọdún ní ìdajì-ọgọ́rùn ọdún; fún ìdí èyí, 77 mílíọ̀nù àwọn wọ̀nyìí ni ó máa jẹ́ nítorí NCDs - àlékún tó fẹ́rẹ̀ súnmọ́ 90% nínú iye ònkà láti ọdún 2019. Ìdúróṣinṣin ìlọsíwájú pè fún amúṣẹ́yá Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tuntun ṣe àfihàn ìfàsẹ́yìn nínú iye àdínkù ọdọọdún (ARR) fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn atọ́ka. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, iye ikú ìyá lágbàyé nílò láti dínkù pẹ̀lú 11.6% ní ọdún kan láàrín ọdún 2021 àti 2030 láti dọ́gba pẹ̀lú àfojúsùn SDG. Bákan náà, àpapọ̀ àdínkù nínú ìṣẹ̀lẹ TB láti ọdún 2015 sí 2021 jẹ́ ìdá-kan-nínú-márùn ọ̀nà láti débi àṣeyọrí 2025 ti Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ Fi Òpin sí TB WHO.
translations.reference: NCDs ̶ an ever-increasing health threat for future generationsDespite overall health progress, the share of deaths caused annually by NCDs has grown consistently and is now claiming nearly three quarters of all lives lost each year. If this trend continues, NCDs are projected to account for about 86% of the 90 million annual deaths by mid-century; consequently, 77 million of these will be due to NCDs – a nearly 90% increase in absolute numbers since 2019. Stagnating progress calls for acceleration More recent trends show signs of slowdown in the annual rate of reduction (ARR) for many indicators. For example, the global maternal mortality ratio needs to decline by 11.6% per year between 2021 and 2030 to meet the SDG target. Similarly, the net reduction in TB incidence from 2015 to 2021 was only one-fifth of the way to the 2025 milestone of WHO’s End TB Strategy.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English.gs:

“NCDs (Non-Communicable Diseases) are increasingly being used as a reference for all annual health reports. The mortality rate of NCDs has been rising steadily over time and has now begun to exceed the death rate in many areas within a decade.  If this trend continues, NCDs are projected to account for approximately 86% to 90% of deaths annually by 2030. To achieve this, 77 million deaths are expected to be due to NCDs – the highest predicted mortality rate of 90% by 2019.  Further development of strategies is needed to address the increasing burden of NCDs. For example, maternal mortality has increased significantly, falling by approximately 11.6% in a single year between 2021 and 2030, aligning with the SDG goals. Consequently, the proportion of NCDs in TB cases from 2015 to 2021 represents a significant deviation from the 2025 WHO TB elimination target.”

**Notes on the translation:**

*   I've used "NCDs (Non-Communicable Diseases)" to clarify the acronym for an English-speaking audience.
*   I aimed for a clear and accurate translation while maintaining the original document's tone and context.
*   I've broken down some of the longer sentences for better readability in English.

translations.source: Pẹ̀lú àdínkù nínú ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú oríṣiríṣi ewu ìlera - bíi lílo tábà, ọtí mímu, rògbòdìyàn, omi tó léwu àti ìmọ́tótó, àti àìdàgbà sókè àwọn ọmọ - ìtẹ̀síwájú náà ò péye bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àwọn ewu kọ̀ọ̀kan bíi bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ sì pọ̀ gan-an. Eyí tó banilẹ́rù ni pé, ọ̀nkà àpapọ ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsan ìsanra ń lékún láì sí ààmì ìdápadà ojú ẹsẹ̀. Síwájú sí i, ìfẹ̀lójú ànfààní sí àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì ti fà sẹ́yìn ní àfiwé àwọn èrè ṣáájú-2015, papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àìsí ìtẹ̀síwájú tó lápẹrẹ nínú àdínkù ìnira ìpèsè owó nítorí iye owó ètò ìlera. Èyí máa mú àdínkù tó lápẹrẹ bá agbára wa láti ṣe àṣeyọrí Ìkárí Ìlera Àgbáyé ní ọdún 2030. Àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 jẹ́ ìrántí pàtàkì pé ìtẹ̀síwájú ò lọ tààrà kò sì ní ìdánilójú," Dr Samira Asma, Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún Dátà, Ìyànnàná àti Àgbékalẹ̀ fún Ipa WHO kílọ̀.
translations.reference: Despite a reduction in exposure to many health risks – such as tobacco use, alcohol consumption, violence, unsafe water and sanitation, and child stunting – progress was inadequate and exposure to some risks such as air pollution remains high. Alarmingly, the prevalence of obesity is rising with no immediate sign of reversal. Furthermore, expanded access to essential health services has slowed compared to pre-2015 gains, coupled with no significant progress in reducing financial hardship due to health-care costs. This drastically limits our ability to achieve Universal Health Coverage by 2030. “The COVID-19 pandemic is an important reminder that progress is neither linear nor guaranteed,” warns Dr Samira Asma, WHO Assistant Director-General for Data, Analytics and Delivery for Impact.

translations.source: "Láti wà lójú ọ̀nà fún ètò SDG ọdún 2030, a gbọ́dọ̀ gbé ìgbésẹ̀ ìpinnu àti lápapọ̀ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ipa tó ṣe é wọ̀n ní gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè." Lára ìjábọ̀ ọdún yìí ni a ti rí fún ìgbà àkọ́kọ́ ìsọ̀rí àkànṣe lórí àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ àti ìlera, a sì ń fojú sọ́nà pé èyí máa tún ṣe pàtàkì nínú ìjábọ̀ náà láti àsìkò yìí lọ. Fún ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ yìí àti àwọn agbègbè mìíràn, dátà ní àsìkò, aṣeégbáralé tí wọ́n yà sọ́tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ ṣe pàtàkì láti tọpa ìlọsíwájú àti láti mú àyípadà bá àwọn òfin ìlera orílẹ̀-èdè èdè àti àgbáyé. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ Olóòtú: Ìjábọ̀ Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé ni àkójọpọ̀ ọdọọdún Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO) fún àwọn dátà tuntun tí ó wà lórí ìlera àti àwọn atọ́ka ajẹmọ́-ìlera. Fún ìbéèrè, kàn sí [ímeèlì tí wọ́n dáàbòbò]
translations.reference: “To stay on track towards the 2030 SDG agenda, we must act decisively and collectively to deliver a measurable impact in all countries.” This year’s report includes for the first time a dedicated section on climate change and health, and we anticipate that this will be of more relevance in the report going forward. For this issue and all other areas timely, reliable and disaggregated data are critical to track progress and improve national and global health policies. Editor’s note: The World Health Statistics report is the World Health Organization’s (WHO) annual compilation of the most recent available data on health and health-related indicators. For inquiries, contact [email protected]

translations.source: Olùdarí-àpapọ̀ WHO kéde Àmì-ẹ̀yẹ Àwọn Olórí Ìlera Lágbàyé Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum àti Peter Piot ni wọ́n fún ní àmì-ẹ̀yẹ fún ìfọkànsìn ìgbésí ayé wọn sí ìlera Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus máa ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àmì-ẹ̀yẹ àṣeyọrí ìgbésí ayé méjì náà láti mọ rírì àfikún aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ sí mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera àgbáyé ní àkókò ìbẹ̀rẹ onípele-gíga fún Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin. Àmì-ẹ̀yẹ Àwọn Olórí Àgbáyé ti Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ náà, tí wọ́n ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní ọdún 2019, máa lọ sí ọ̀dọ Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum àti Peter Piot. Àwọn méjèèjì tó gba àmì-ẹ̀yẹ yìí jẹ́ onímọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì àti olórí ìlera àwùjọ tó yàtọ̀ tí wọ́n jọ ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ nínú ìṣàwárí ààrun kòkòrò Ẹ̀bọ́là ṣíwájú kí wọ́n tó dé ipò aṣíwájú nínú ìlera àgbáyé.
translations.reference: WHO Director-General announces Global Health Leaders Awards Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot awarded for lifelong commitment to health WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus will present two lifetime achievement awards to recognize outstanding contributions to advancing global health during the high-level opening session of the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly. The Director-General’s Global Leaders Awards, which were established in 2019, will go to Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot. Both recipients are distinguished scientists and public health leaders who were closely involved in the discovery of Ebola virus disease before advancing to leadership positions in global health.

translations.source: Àmì-ẹ̀yẹ náà bu iyì fún àṣeyọrí ìgbésí ayé wọn nínú ìlera àwùjọ. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Muyembe-Tamfum láti Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) ni wọ́n ń bu ọlá fún fún ìmọ̀-ọ̀tun, aṣíwájú àti àwọn ìdásí tó lápẹẹrẹ rẹ̀ sí àmójútó àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ààrùn bíi ààrun kòkòrò Ẹ̀bólà. Ó ti kó ipa pàtàkì nínú mímú àyípadà bá àbájáde ìlera ní Democratic Republic of Congo, ní ẹkùn agbègbè ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, àti ní àwùjọ àgbáyé. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Muyembe-Tamfum ni Olùdarí Àpapọ̀ fún National Institute of Biomedical Research ti DRC ní Kinshasa, Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n tààrà fún ní University of Kinshasa Medical School àti ààrẹ àkọ́kọ́ fún Congolese Academy of Science. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Muyembe-Tamfum wà lára ikọ̀ tí wọ́n ṣàwarí kòkòrò ẹ̀bólà Zaïre ní àkókò tí wọ́n kọ́kọ́ ní àkọsílẹ̀ ìtànkálẹ̀ náà ní ọdún 1976, ní Equateur Province, Central DRC.
translations.reference: The award honours their lifetime achievements in public health. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) is being recognized for his innovation, leadership and ground-breaking contributions to the management and treatment of diseases such as Ebola virus disease. He has played a critical role in improving health outcomes in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, the African region, and the global community. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum is the Director General of the DRC’s National Institute of Biomedical Research in Kinshasa, full Professor of Microbiology at the University of Kinshasa Medical School and the inaugural president of the Congolese Academy of Science. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum was part of the team that discovered the Zaïre ebolavirus during the first recorded outbreak in 1976, in Equateur Province, Central DRC.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

“This medical research is highly valued for its contribution to the advancement of healthcare knowledge. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum is being honored by the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) for his expertise in virology, epidemiology, and related areas, including specific diseases like Ebola. He has made a significant contribution to the fight against disease in the DRC, within the regional Adulawa initiative and in international collaborations. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum is the Director of the National Institute of Biomedical Research in DRC in Kinshasa, a Professor Emeritus at the University of Kinshasa Medical School, and a corresponding member of the Congolese Academy of Science. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum was among the first researchers who studied Ebola in Zaire, as documented in the first report published in 1976 in the Equateur Province, Central DRC.”

translations.source: Ó tún padà ṣe ìrànwọ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ọ̀kan lára àwọn ìtọ́jú tó ṣiṣẹ́ jùlọ kojú kòkòrò ààrùn náà àti ṣíṣe àmúlò àyẹ̀wo àwọn àjẹsára kòkòrò ààrùn Ẹ̀bólà. Ó ti ṣiṣẹ́ láì sinmi láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìkápá ajẹmọ́-sáyẹ́nsì Democratic Republic of Congo, tí ó ń ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ oríṣiríṣi àwọ̀ ààyè fún ìwádìí ní orílẹ̀-èdè náà, ó sì ti kó ipa pàtàkì pẹ̀lú WHO nínú ìgbógun ti àwọn ààrùn àkóràn. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Piot láti orílẹ̀-èdè Belgium ni wọ́n ń bu ọlá fún fún aṣíwájú aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ rẹ̀ nínú ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn KASA àti àwọn ìpèníjà ìlera àgbáyé mìíràn tó ṣe pàtàkì. Ó ti kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ìfilọ́lẹ̀ àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ ìlera àgbáyé tó ṣe pàtàkì bíi UNAIDS, Global Fund, àti àwọn mìíràn tí wọ́n ti mú àyípadà bá àwọn àbájáde ìlera káàkiri àgbáyé. Lábẹ́ ìdarí rẹ̀, UNAIDS di agbẹnusọ àgbà fún ìgbésẹ̀ jákèjádò àgbáyé takò KASA, tó sì tún léwájú àtúntò UN pẹ̀lu ṣíṣe àkójọpọ̀ àwọn àjọ UN 10 ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀.
translations.reference: He later helped design one of the first effective treatments against the virus and the deployment of experimental Ebola virus disease vaccines. He has worked tirelessly to build the Democratic Republic of the Congo’s scientific capacity, establishing multiple research facilities in the country, and has played key roles with WHO in fighting infectious diseases. Professor Piot from Belgium is being recognized for his extraordinary leadership in the response to the HIV/AIDS epidemic and other significant global health challenges. He has played a vital role in launching major global health initiatives such as UNAIDS, the Global Fund, and others that have improved health outcomes around the world. Under his leadership, UNAIDS became the chief advocate for worldwide action against AIDS, also spearheading UN reform by bringing together 10 different UN organizations.

translations.source: Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Piot tún ti kó ipa nínú ìṣàwárí kòkòrò ààrùn Ẹ̀bólà ní ìbẹ̀rẹ̀, àti kíkápá ìtànkálẹ̀ àkọ́kọ́ tí a mọ̀, tó sì ń kópa nínú àwọn ìwádìí ẹ̀kọ́ àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ní ààyè ìwádìí. Ó ti jẹ́ alága fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ onípele-gíga, kópa nínú àìmọye àwọn ipò olùbánidámọ́ràn onípele-gíga, tó fi mọ́ COVID-19, ó sì ti gba ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àmì-ẹ̀yẹ. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Piot ni Olùbánidámọ́ràn Pàtàkì fún Ààrẹ EC, Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n nínú Ìlera Àgbáyé àti Olùdarí tẹ́lẹ̀ fún London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine. "Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n tayọ wọ̀nyìí jẹ́ àpẹẹrẹ ìfọkànsìn ayérayé, agbẹnusọ aláìdáwọ́dúró, ìfọkànsìn fún ìdọ́gba, àti iṣẹ́ àìnímọtara-ẹni-nìkan fún ọmọnìyàn àti ìlera àwọn ènìyàn," Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO, Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. translations.source: WHO kí orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali kú oríire fún Ìṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìṣòro ìlera àwujọ WHO ti dá orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali láre pé wọ́n ti ṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìṣòro ìlera àwujọ, tó sọ wọ́n di àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè karùn-ún àti ìkẹfà ní Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ WHO láti ṣe àṣeyọrí aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ yìí. Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ti gba ìdáláre WHO tẹ́lẹ̀ fún ìṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ni orílẹ̀-èdè Ghana (oṣù Òkudù ọdún 2018), Gambia (oṣù Igbe ọdún 2021), Togo (òṣì Èbìbí ọdún 2022), àti Malawi (oṣù Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2022). "WHO kí àwọn aláṣẹ ìlera fún orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali àti àwọn ojú òpó wọn fún àwọn àjọṣepọ̀ àgbáyé àti abẹ́lé kú oríire fún àwọn àṣeyọrí wọ̀nyìí", Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Lẹ́yìn àṣeyọrí orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali, ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ṣì dúró gẹ́gẹ́ bíi àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 23 ní Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ WHO, tó ń mú wa tẹ̀síwájú súnmọ́ àfojúsùn ìṣẹ́gun fún ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú tí wọ́n ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ nínú ìlànà-iṣẹ́ fún àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì 2021-2030.
translations.reference: WHO congratulates Benin and Mali for eliminating trachoma as a public health problem WHO has validated Benin and Mali as having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem, making them the fifth and sixth countries in WHO’s African Region to achieve this significant milestone. Countries that previously received WHO validation for trachoma elimination are Ghana (June 2018), Gambia (April 2021), Togo (May 2022) and Malawi (September 2022). “WHO congratulates the health authorities of Benin and Mali and their network of global and local partners for these milestones”, said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “Following Benin’s and Mali’s success, trachoma remains endemic in 23 countries in WHO’s African Region, bringing us a step closer towards the elimination target for trachoma set in the road map for neglected tropical diseases 2021–2030.”
translations.hypothesis:

WHO urged Benin and Mali to strengthen their efforts to combat malaria as a priority health program, following a commitment made by WHO to Benin and Mali that they had strengthened malaria control efforts as a priority health program, making them resilient countries and contributing to the WHO Regional Office’s efforts to provide this support. The countries that have received initial WHO funding for malaria control are Ghana (in 2018), Gambia (in 2021), Togo (in November 2022), and Malawi (in September 2022). “WHO asks health officials in Benin and Mali, and their counterparts, to strengthen their partnerships with international and global organizations for these initiatives,” said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. “Following the support to Benin and Mali, malaria control is being implemented firmly as a flagship program in 23 countries within the WHO Regional Office, leading us to a more coordinated approach to strengthening the efforts they are undertaking in their global health initiatives for the 2021-2030 period.”

translations.source: Ní àgbáyé, orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali darapọ̀ mọ́ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 15 mìíràn tí wọ́n ti gba ìdáláre láti ọ̀dọ WHO fún ìṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìṣòro ìlera àwujọ. Àwọn náà ni Cambodia, China, Gambia, Ghana, Islamic Republic of Iran, Lao People’s Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo àti Vanuatu. Ìṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú pẹ̀lu lílo Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ SAFE WHO Àpapọ orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali ṣàmúlò Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ SAFE tí WHO dábàá láti ṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú pẹ̀lú àtìlẹyìn WHO àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀. Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ SAFE náà ni àkójọpọ̀ iṣẹ́-abẹ láti ṣe ìtọ́jú àwọn ìnira ìgbẹ̀yin àwọn ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú; oògùn apa kòkòro batéríà láti mú àkóràn kúrò; ìmọ́tótó ojú, àti àyípadà agbègbè, pàápàá jùlọ mímú àyípadà bá ànfààní sí omi àti ìmọ́tótó, láti mú àdínkù bá ìtànkálẹ̀.
translations.reference: Globally, Benin and Mali join 15 other countries that have been validated by WHO for having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem. These are Cambodia, China, the Gambia, Ghana, Islamic Republic of Iran, Lao People’s Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo and Vanuatu. Trachoma elimination using WHO’s SAFE strategy Both Benin and Mali implemented the WHO-recommended SAFE strategy to eliminate trachoma with the support of WHO and partners. The SAFE strategy consists of surgery to treat late trachoma complications; antibiotics to clear infection; facial cleanliness; and environmental improvement, particularly improving access to water and sanitation, to reduce transmission.

“Worldwide, Benin and Mali have joined the 15 countries that received funding from the WHO for their efforts in combating infectious diseases as a global health initiative. These countries include Cambodia, China, Gambia, Ghana, Islamic Republic of Iran, Lao People’s Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo, and Vanuatu.

The SAFE Health Initiative, which is a joint effort to identify and implement infectious disease control measures, was launched by Benin and Mali in collaboration with the WHO and partners.

The SAFE Health Initiative is a comprehensive service to identify infectious disease control measures; it uses battery-powered sensors to make the color dark; eye scans, and regional awareness, to greatly increase awareness of water and disease, to make the solution a clear story.”

translations.source: Nípasẹ̀ Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè, oògùn apa kòkòro batéríà azithromycin ni Pfizer máa ń gbékalẹ̀ fún àwọn ètò ìṣẹ́gun tí wọ́n ń ṣe àmúlò ìlànà-iṣẹ́ SAFE náà. Orílẹ̀-èdè Benin ti ṣe àmúlò àwọn ìdásí ìṣẹ́gùn ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú pẹ̀lú àwọn tí wọ́n ṣe àmúlò kojú àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì mìíràn (NTDs), lábẹ́ àbùradà Ètò Orílẹ̀-èdè fún Àwọn Ààrùn Àkóràn. Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ni NTD kẹ́ta tí wọ́n máa ṣẹ́gun ní orílẹ̀-èdè Benin, lẹ́yin sòbìà (ní ọdún 2009) àti ààrun sunrun-sunrun (ní ọdún 2021). Orílẹ̀-èdè Mali ti ṣe àwọn ìwádìí ipa àti ìtọpinpin ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ó sì ti ṣe àgbéjáde àwọn ìdásí láti ṣe àṣeyọrí àwọn àfojúsùn ìṣẹ́gun, pẹ̀lú àwọn ìpèníjà ààbò ní àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè àríwá orílẹ̀-èdè náà àti àwọn ìdàrúdápọ̀ òṣèlú-àwùjọ láti bíi ọdún díẹ̀ sẹ́yìn. Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ni NTD àkọ́kọ́ tí wọ́n máa ṣẹ́gun ní orílẹ̀-èdè Mali, tí ó ti wá darapọ̀ báyìí mọ́ àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àgbáyé fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 47 tí wọ́n ti ṣẹ́gun ó kéré jù NTD kan.
translations.reference: Through the International Trachoma Initiative, the antibiotic azithromycin is donated by Pfizer to elimination programmes implementing the SAFE strategy. Benin has integrated trachoma elimination interventions with those implemented against other neglected tropical diseases (NTDs), under the umbrella of the National Programme for Communicable Diseases. Trachoma is the third NTD to be eliminated in Benin, after dracunculiasis (in 2009) and gambiense human African trypanosomiasis (in 2021). Mali has conducted trachoma impact and surveillance surveys and rolled out interventions to achieve elimination targets, despite security challenges in the northern regions of the country and sociopolitical upheavals in recent years. Trachoma is the first NTD to be eliminated in Mali, which therefore now joins a global group of 47 countries that have eliminated at least one NTD.

“In the face of the schistosomiasis epidemic in the Okèèrè region, the Pfizer company is providing azithromycin tablets for the tests they conduct in order to implement the SAFE protocol. The Republic of Benin has initiated schistosomiasis control measures, including those they conduct to identify and distribute NTDs (neglected tropical diseases), such as the World Health Organization’s program. Schistosomiasis is the most prevalent NTD in Benin, following malaria (in 2009) and other recurring diseases (in 2021). The Republic of Mali has also undertaken activities and initiatives related to schistosomiasis control, developing measures to support surveillance systems, with grants awarded to regional organizations within the country and digital data collection to track progress over time. Schistosomiasis is the first NTD prevalent in Mali, which has now joined the global group of countries that have surpassed a single NTD disease. ”

translations.source: "Àwọn wọ̀nyìí jẹ́ àwọn àṣeyọrí ìlera àwùjọ tó yanilẹ́nu," Dr Ibrahim Socé Fall, Olùdarí Ètò NTD Lágbàyé WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali ṣe àpèjúwe bí èròngbà òṣèlú tó lágbára, ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ ẹlẹ́ka-jẹ̀ka, ìtọpinpin àti ìfọ̀rọ̀wérò àwùjọ lè ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ìṣẹ́gun ààrùn." Ìtẹ̀síwájú tó lápẹẹrẹ ti di ṣíṣe nínú ìgbógun ti ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú láti bíi ọdún mélòó kan sẹ́yìn. Iye àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n nílò ìtọ́jú oògùn apa kòkòro batéríà fún ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ní Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ WHO ti dínkù pẹ̀lú 84 mílíọ̀nù, láti 189 mílíọ̀nù ní ọdún 2014 sí 105 mílíọ̀nù ní oṣù Òkudù ọdún 2022. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ fún olóòtú: Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ṣì dúró gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 41 (títí di oṣù Òkudù ọdún 2022) pẹ̀lú àpapọ 125 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn agbègbè tó nílò ìdásí kojú ààrùn náà.
translations.reference: “These are impressive public health achievements,” said Dr Ibrahima Socé Fall, Director of the WHO Global NTD Programme. “Benin and Mali demonstrate how strong political will, cross-sector integration, surveillance and community engagement can work in concert to achieve disease elimination.” Significant progress has been made in the fight against trachoma over the past few years. The number of people requiring antibiotic treatment for trachoma in the WHO African Region fell by 84 million, from 189 million in 2014 to 105 million as of June 2022. Note to the editor: Trachoma remains a public health problem in 41 countries (as of June 2022) with an estimated 125 million people living in areas requiring interventions against the disease.

The number of people who participated in the global malaria battery training for the reduction strategy reached 84 million in 2014, rising to 189 million in December 2022, and decreasing to 105 million in September 2022. A key message for the public: The reduction strategy continues to be implemented as a contribution to public health in 41 countries (until December 2022) with a total of 125 million people benefiting from the areas where malaria is prevalent.”

translations.source: A máa ń rí ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ní àwọn agbègbè tí wọ́n kúṣẹ̀ tí sì luko jùlọ ní Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, Central àti South America, Asia, Western Pacific àti Middle East. Àwọn Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ WHO ni ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ṣàkóbá fún jùlọ pẹ̀lú 105 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn agbègbè tó kángun séwu, tí ó dúró fún 84% gbogbo ìṣòro ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ní àgbáyé. Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ni ni àkóràn tó ń fa ìfọ́jú tó ń léwájú jákèjádò àgbáyé. Àkóràn pẹ̀lú batéríà aṣojú ààrùn ìbálòpọ̀ ló ń fa ààrùn náà. Àkóràn máa ń ràn láti ara ẹnìkan sí ẹnìkan nípasẹ àwọn ìka tó ti dọ̀tí, agbé kòkòrò pamọ́ àti àwọn eṣinṣin tí wọ́n ti fi ara kínra pẹ̀lú oje láti ojú tàbí imú ẹni tí ó ti ní ààrùn náà.
translations.reference: Trachoma is found mainly in the poorest and most rural areas of Africa, Central and South America, Asia, the Western Pacific and the Middle East. The WHO African Region is disproportionately affected by trachoma with 105 million people living in at-risk areas, which represents 84% of the global trachoma burden. Trachoma is the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide. The disease is caused by infection with the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection is transmitted from person to person through contaminated fingers, fomites and flies that have come into contact with discharge from the eyes or nose of an infected person.

“We are seeing a significant increase in preventable diseases in regions where people are dying and where there is a high concentration in Central and South America, Asia, the Western Pacific, and the Middle East. The Global Health Organization’s Regional Health Authorities are working with approximately 105 million people in these vulnerable areas, which account for 84% of the global burden of preventable diseases. Preventable diseases are the primary cause of mortality worldwide.  The main factor contributing to this mortality is the lack of access to vaccines.  The disease spreads from person to person through various routes, including direct contact, contaminated water, and goods that are packed closely together with labels that obscure the information for those who have a disease. ”

translations.source: Àwọn aṣokùnfà ewu àwùjọ fún ìtànkálẹ̀ ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ni ìmọ́tótó tí kò dára, àwọn ilé elérò púpọ̀, àìní ànfààní tí ó tó sí omi tàbí lílo àwọn ohun èlò ìmọ́tótó tí ó tọ́. Ní ọdún 1996, WHO ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ Ìṣọ̀kan WHO fún Ìṣẹ́gun Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ní Àgbáyé ní ọdún 2020 (GET2020). Pẹ̀lú àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ tuntun nínú Ìṣọ̀kan náà, WHO ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ṣíṣe àmúlò ìlànà-iṣẹ́ SAFE ní orílẹ̀-èdè àti ìrólágbára ìkápá orílẹ̀-èdè nípasẹ àyẹ̀wo ajẹmọ́ àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn, àmójútó, ìtọpinpin , ìgbéléwọ̀n iṣẹ́-àkànṣe àti àkójọpọ̀ ohun èlò tó ń ṣe àfikún sí ìṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìṣòro ìlera àwujọ. Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ náà, tí Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé ti ọdún 2020 fọwọ́sí náà nípasẹ̀ ìpinnu rẹ̀ 73(33), mú ọdún 2030 gẹ́gẹ́ bíi déétì àfojúsùn tuntun fún ìṣẹ́gùn àgbáyé.
translations.reference: Environmental risk factors for trachoma transmission include poor hygiene, overcrowded households, inadequate access to water or use of proper sanitation facilities. In 1996, WHO launched the WHO Alliance for the Global Elimination of Trachoma by the year 2020 (GET2020). With other partners in the Alliance, WHO supports country implementation of the SAFE strategy and strengthening of national capacity through epidemiological assessment, monitoring, surveillance, project evaluation and resource mobilization contributing towards elimination of trachoma as a public health problem. The road map, endorsed by the World Health Assembly in 2020 through its decision 73(33), sets 2030 as the new target date for global elimination.

This operational model, proposed by the 2020 Global Commission in its Resolution 73(33), sets 2030 as a deadline for global goals."

translations.source: 152 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìkókó ni wọ́n bí ṣíwájú àkókò láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn Nínú gbogbo àwọn ìkókó 10 tí wọ́n bá bí, 1 jẹ́ síwájú àkókò - bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ní gbogbo 40 ìṣẹ́jú àáyá, 1 nínú àwọn ìkókó yẹn máa ń kú Iye ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ò tíì yí padà láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn ní èyíkéyìí ẹkùn agbègbè ní àgbáyé Àwọn ipa rògbòdìyàn, àyípadà ojú ọjọ́, COVID-19 ń mú àlékún bá ewu fún àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ìkókó níbi gbogbo Ìgbésẹ̀ ni a nílò lójú ẹsẹ̀ láti mú àyípadà bá ìdènà ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, papọ̀ pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú tó dára fún àwọn ìkókó tí ọ̀rọ́ kàn àti àwọn ẹbí wọn
translations.reference: 152 million babies born preterm in the last decade Of every 10 babies born, 1 is preterm – and every 40 seconds, 1 of those babies dies Preterm birth rates have not changed in the past decade in any region of the world The impacts of conflict, climate change, COVID-19 are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere Action is needed urgently to improve the prevention of preterm birth, alongside better care for affected babies and their families

translations.source: Àpapọ 13.4 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìkókó ni wọ́n bí ṣíwájú àkókò ní ọdún 2020, tí bíi 1 mílíọ̀nù sì kú látara ìnira ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìjábọ̀ tuntun kan tí àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé gbé jáde lónì. Èyí dọ́gba pẹ̀lú bíi 1 nínú àwọn ìkókó 10 tí wọ́n tètè bí (ṣíwájú ọ̀sẹ 37 ìlóyún) jákèjádò àgbáyé. Tí wọ́n tètè bí: ọdún mẹ́wàá iṣẹ́ lórí ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, tí Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO) àti Ìpèsè Owó fún Àwọn Ọmọdé ti Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé (UNICEF) gbé jáde pẹ̀lú PMNCH -- ìṣọ̀kan tó tóbi jùlọ ní àgbáyé fún àwọn obìnrin, àwọn ọmọde, àti àwọn ọ̀dọ́, ṣe ìkìlọ̀ lórí "pàjáwírì ìdákẹ́rọ́rọ́" fún ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, tí wọn ò fiyẹ̀sí-púpọ̀ tipẹ́ nínú ìwọ̀n àti agbára rẹ̀, tí ó ń ṣe ìdíwọ́ fún ìlọsíwájú nínú ìmú àyípadà bá ìlera àti ìwàláyé àwọn ọmọdé. Lára ìjábọ̀ náà ni a ti rí àwọn àkójọpọ̀ láti ọ̀dọ WHO àti UNICEF, tí wọ́n ṣe pẹ̀lú London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, lórí ọ̀nkà àpapọ ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsan ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò. Lápapọ̀, ó ṣàwarí rẹ̀ pé iye ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ò tíì yí padà ní ẹkùn agbègbè kankan ní àgbáyé láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn, pẹ̀lú 152 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìkókó tí wọ́n tètè bí tó kángun séwu láti ọdún 2010 sí 2020.
translations.reference: An estimated 13.4 million babies were born pre-term in 2020, with nearly 1 million dying from preterm complications, according to a new report released by United Nations agencies and partners today. This is equivalent to around 1 in 10 babies born early (before 37 weeks of pregnancy) worldwide. Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth, produced by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF) together with PMNCH -- the world’s largest alliance for women, children, and adolescents, sounds the alarm on a “silent emergency” of preterm birth, long under-recognized in its scale and severity, which is impeding progress in improving children’s health and survival. The report includes updated estimates from WHO and UNICEF, prepared with the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, on the prevalence of preterm births. Overall, it finds that preterm birth rates have not changed in any region in the world in the past decade, with 152 million vulnerable babies born too soon from 2010 to 2020.

Specifically, the World Health Organization (WHO) and UNICEF, in collaboration with PMNCH, addressed the issue of “preventable deaths” during childbirth, which were still prevalent in many countries, leading to criticism and calls for improved maternal and child health services.  We have seen collaborations with the WHO and UNICEF, alongside the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, to conduct research on newborn birth initiatives. translations.source: Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ti di aṣokùnfà ikú àwọn ọmọdé tó ń léwájú, tó dúró fún 1 nínú 5 lé gbogbo ikú àwọn ọmọdé tó ń ṣẹlẹ̀ ṣíwájú ọjọ́-ìbí 5th. Àwọn alájàbọ́ ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò lè kojú àwọn ìpadàbọ̀ ìlera títí ayérayé, pẹ̀lú àlékún nínú ìpèníjà ara àti àwọn ìdíwọ́ ìdàgbàsókè tó ṣe é ṣe. Àwọn àlàfo àjàbọ́ nípasẹ̀ ẹkùn agbègbè, ìpawówọlé, ìran Gígùn lórí ìjábọ̀ tó lápẹẹrẹ lórí àkòrí-ọ̀rọ̀ náà ní ọdún 2012, ìjábọ̀ "ọlọ́dún mẹ́wàá" Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò tuntun yìí pèsè ìwòye tó kún nípa ọ̀nkà àpapọ ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsan ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò àti ipa rẹ̀ tó lápẹẹrẹ lára àwọn obìnrin, àwọn ẹbí, àwọn àwùjọ àti àwọn ọrọ̀-ajé. Ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà, ibi tí wọ́n bí àwọn ìkókó sí máa ń sọ bóyá wọn ó ja àjàbọ́.
translations.reference: Preterm birth is now the leading cause of child deaths, accounting for more than 1 in 5 of all deaths of children occurring before their 5th birthday. Preterm survivors can face lifelong health consequences, with an increased likelihood of disability and developmental delays. Survival gaps by region, income, race Building from a landmark report on the topic in 2012, this new Born too soon “decade” report provides a comprehensive overview of the prevalence of preterm birth and its profound impact on women, families, societies and economies. Too often, where babies are born determines if they survive.

translations.source: Ìjábọ̀ náà sọ wí pé 1 nínú 10 nìkan nínú àwọn ìkókó oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò tó lágbára (<28 ọ̀sẹ̀) máa ń ja àjàbọ́ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré, yàtọ̀ sí 9 nínú 10 lé ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn pọ̀. Àwọn àlàfo àìṣedéédé tó jọ mọ́ ìran, ẹ̀yà, ìpawówọlé, àti ànfààní sí ìlera ojúlówó máa ń sọ ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, ikú, àti ìpèníjà ara tó ṣe é ṣe, kódà ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn pọ̀. Southern Asia àti ìwọ̀-oọ̀rùn ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ ni wọ́n ní iye ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò tó pọ̀ jùlọ, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ìkókó ṣíwájú àkókò ìbí ní àwọn agbègbè wọ̀nyìí ń kojú ewu ikú tó pọ̀ jùlọ. Lápapọ̀, àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè méjì wọ̀nyìí dúró fún 65% lé àwọn ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò káàkiri àgbáyé. Ìjábọ̀ náà tún ṣàfihàn pé àwọn ipa rògbòdìyàn, àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ àti ìbàjẹ́ àwùjọ, COVID-19, àti àlékún nínú owó iná ń mú àlékún bá ewu fún àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ìkókó níbi gbogbo.
translations.reference: The report notes that only 1 in 10 extremely preterm babies (<28 weeks) survive in low-income countries, compared to more than 9 in 10 in high-income countries. Gaping inequalities related to race, ethnicity, income, and access to quality care determine the likelihood of preterm birth, death, and disability, even in high-income countries. Southern Asia and sub-Saharan Africa have the highest rates of preterm birth, and preterm babies in these regions face the highest mortality risk. Together, these two regions account for more than 65% of preterm births globally. The report also highlights that the impacts of conflict, climate change and environmental damage, COVID-19, and rising living costs are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere.

“Researchers have found that among infants aged under 28 weeks (<28 ọ̀sẹ̀), a significant proportion of babies experience high birth weight. This proportion decreases as the country’s socioeconomic status increases, dropping to 9 out of 10 in countries with higher socioeconomic status. Factors such as maternal education, ethnicity, socioeconomic status, and nutrition are associated with increased birth weight and are linked to premature birth, infant mortality, and child poverty. Southern Asia and Southeast Asia have the highest rates of increased birth weight babies.  Specifically, these two regions account for 65% of globally increased birth weight babies. The report also highlights that factors such as deforestation, solar radiation, and global trade, including the COVID-19 pandemic and financial instability, contribute to increased mortality rates for women and babies worldwide.”

translations.source: Fún àpẹẹrẹ, bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ ni wọ́n ní ó ń ṣokùnfà 6 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan. Bíi 1 nínú 10 àwọn ìkókó oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ni wọ́n bí ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 10 tí wọ́n lẹ jùlọ tí rògbòdìyàn ọmọnìyàn ní ṣẹlẹ̀ níbẹ̀, gẹ́gẹ́ bí àlàyé tuntun kan nínú ìjábọ̀ náà. Àwọn ewu ìlera ìyá, bíi ìlóyún ọ̀dọ́ àti ẹ̀jẹ̀-ríru, ni wọ́n tún ní àjọṣepọ̀ tó gúnmọ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò. Ẹ̀yí tọ́ka sí ìdí láti ṣe àrídájú ànfààní sí àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìbímọ, tó fi mọ́ ìfètòsọ́mọ-bíbí tó ń ṣiṣẹ́, pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú ojúlówó-ńlá nínú oyún àti ní àkókò ìbí. Ètò fún iṣẹ́: àlékún ìdókówò orílẹ̀-èdè àti ìpolongo tí àwọn òbí léwájú rẹ̀
translations.reference: For example, air pollution is estimated to contribute to 6 million preterm births each year. Nearly 1 in 10 preterm babies are born in the 10 most fragile countries affected by humanitarian crises, according to a new analysis in the report. Maternal health risks, such as adolescent pregnancy and pre-eclampsia, are closely linked to preterm births. This underlines the need to ensure access to sexual and reproductive health services, including effective family planning, with high-quality care in pregnancy and around the time of birth. Agenda for action: more country investment and parent-led activism

translations.source: Ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn náà tún ti rí ìdagbàsókè nínú ìpolongo àwùjọ lórí ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò àti ìdènà àbíkú, tí àjọṣepọ̀ àwọn òbí, àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ìlera, àwọn ilé-ẹ̀kọ́, àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ, àti àwọn mìíràn ń léwájú rẹ̀. Jákèjádò àgbáńlá ayé, àwọn ẹgbẹ́ fún àwọn ẹbí oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò tí ọ̀rọ́ kàn ti ń léwájú tí wọ́n ń fún ìpèsè fún ànfààní sí ìtọ́jú tó dára àti àyípadà òfin àti àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ẹbí mìíràn. Ní ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún Àpérò Ìlera ọmọtuntun àti Ìyá, ní Cape Town, ní orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa láti 8-11 oṣù Èbìbí, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA àti PMNCH ń pè fún àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ wọ̀nyìí láti mú àyípadà bá ìtọ́jú fún àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ọmọtuntun àti láti dènà àwọn ewu látara àwọn ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò: Àlékún àwọn ìdókówò: Ṣíṣe àkójọ àwọn ohun èlò ilẹ̀-òkèèrè àti lábẹ́lé láti ṣàmúlò ìlera ìyá àti ọmọ tuntun, ṣíṣe àrídájú pé ojúlówó ìtọ́jú ńlá di ṣíṣe ní ìgbà àti ibi tí wọ́n bá ti nílò rẹ̀. Ìṣàmúlò kíákíá: Ṣíṣe àwọn àfojúsùn orílẹ̀-èdè fún ìtẹ̀síwájú nípasẹ ìṣàmúlò àwọn òfin orílẹ̀-èdè tó ti wà tẹ́lẹ̀ fún ìtọ́jú ìyá àti ọmọ tuntun.
translations.reference: The past decade has also seen a growth of community activism on preterm birth and stillbirth prevention, driven by networks of parents, health professionals, academia, civil society, and others. Throughout the world, groups for affected families of preterm birth have been at the forefront of advocating for access to better care and policy change and supporting other families. Leading up to the International Maternal Newborn Health Conference, in Cape Town, South Africa from 8-11 May, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA and PMNCH are calling for the following actions to improve care for women and newborns and mitigate risks from preterm births: Increased investments: Mobilizing international and domestic resources to optimize maternal and newborn health, ensuring high quality care is provided when and where it is needed. Accelerated implementation: Meeting country targets for progress through implementing established national policies for maternal and newborn care.

“In the fourth year, a significant achievement was realized in the health sector regarding adolescent health and safe motherhood, with participation from midwives, healthcare professionals, schools, community organizations, and individuals seeking to access it.  Specifically, groups for adolescent health initiatives, with a focus on the information being disseminated, are seeking guidance and adherence to proper standards and ethical practices for individuals.  In a preliminary announcement for the Young Health Professionals and Mothers, in Cape Town, South Africa, from October 8th to 11th, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA, and PMNCH are requesting these events to provide guidance for the benefit of women and children, and to address harmful practices related to adolescent health.  Key recommendations: Implement standardized quality control measures and training programs for maternal and child health services, reduce the number of healthcare providers who are not practicing and where they are located.  Demonstration activities: Implement national campaigns for recruitment through demonstrations of established international standards for maternal and child health information. ”

translations.source: Àfikún yíká àwọn ẹ̀ka: Ṣíṣe àtìlẹyìn fún ètò-ẹ̀kọ́ nípasẹ ìṣẹ̀mí náà; ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ìdókówò ọrọ̀-ajé tó já fáfá, pẹ̀lú ìpèsè-owó-àjọṣe káàkiri àwọn ẹ̀ka; ríró àwọn ìdáhùn ìfaradà ojú ọjọ́ lágbára kárí ìgbésí-ayé; àti mímú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ìṣàkóso àti ìfaradà àwọn èto pàjáwírì. Ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun tó ń wáyé lábẹ́lé: Ìdókówò nínú àwọn ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun àti ìwádìí tó ń wáyé lábẹ́lé láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn àyípadà nínú ìjẹ́ ojúlówó ìtọ́jú àti ìdọ́gba nínú ànfààní. Àgbàsọ: Prof. Joy Lawn, Ile-ẹ̀kọ́ fún Ìmọ́tótó àti Ìmọ̀-Ìṣègùn ti London Aṣíwájú-àjọṣe, Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò: ọdún mẹ́wàá iṣẹ́ lórí ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò (2023) àti Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò (2012) "Ìjábọ̀ tuntun yìí ṣe àfihàn pé èrè àìgbé ìgbésẹ̀ láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn ni 152 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìkókó oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò.
translations.reference: Integration across sectors: Promoting education through the life cycle; supporting smarter economic investments, with co-financing across sectors; strengthening climate adaptation responses across the life-course; and advancing the coordination and resilience of emergency systems. Locally driven innovation: Investing in locally led innovation and research to support improvements in quality of care and equity in access. Quotes: Prof. Joy Lawn, London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine Co-lead, Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth (2023) and Born too soon (2012) "This new report shows that the cost of inaction over the last decade was 152 million babies born too soon.

translations.source: Nígbà tí ó kan àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè kan púpọ̀, ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ń dúkokò mọ́ ìlọsíwájú ìlera ní gbogbo orílẹ̀-èdè. Ìdókòwò tó pọ̀ nínú ìtọ́jú fún àwọn ọmọtuntun akángun séwu lè dóólà mílíọ̀nù gbé mílíọ̀nù àwọn ẹbí níbi ìbanilọ́kànjẹ́ náà. Ó tún nílò ọ̀pọ iṣẹ́ láti dènà ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, tí yóò tún mú àyípadà bá ìlọsíwájú nínú mímú àdínkù bá àbíkú àti ikú ìyá. Lápapọ̀ àwọn ìtọpinpin oníbejì fún ìdènà ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò àti ìtọ́jú máa pèsè àwọn ènìyàn àti àwọn àwùjọ tí wọ́n ní àlàáfíà láti ṣiṣẹ́ lórí ìdàgbàsókè ọrọ̀-ajé àti àwùjọ. Àwọn ìran wa tó ń bọ̀ gbáralé kí gbogbo wa gbé ìgbésẹ̀ báyìí -- ìdókówò náà lè má kéré ṣùgbọ́n àwọn ère lórí ìdókówò yìí máa ṣe pàtàkì fún gbogbo orílẹ̀-èdè "
translations.reference: While some regions are more affected, preterm birth threatens health progress in every country. Greater investment in the care of vulnerable newborns can save millions of families from heartbreak. More work is also needed to prevent preterm birth, which will also improve progress in reducing stillbirths and maternal deaths. Together these twin tracks of preterm prevention and care will produce healthier individuals and societies to deliver on economic and social development. Our next generation depends on us all acting now -- the investment may not be small but the returns on this investment will be major for every country"

translations.source: Dr Nahya Salim, Tanzania: ònkọ̀wé-àjọṣe Born Too Soon àti adarí orílẹ̀-èdè fún Newborn Essential Solutions and Technologies 360 (NEST360) "Kò wá sí àwíjàre báyìí fún idákẹ́ rọ́rọ́. Ọdún mẹ́wàá tó ń bọ̀ lè ó sì gbọdọ̀ yàtọ̀ fún àwọn tó ń kojú ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò níbí àti ní ibi gbogbo."
translations.reference: Dr Nahya Salim, Tanzania: Born Too Soon co-author and national lead of Newborn Essential Solutions and Technologies 360 (NEST360) “There is now no excuse for silence. I am proud to see my government now investing and committed to implementation across even rural areas. The next decade can and must be different for those facing preterm birth here and everywhere.”

translations.source: Pẹ̀lú gbogbo ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ itẹ̀síwájú tí gbogbo ayé ti ṣe láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn, a ò tíì ní ìlọsíwájú kankan láti mú àdínkù bá iye àwọn ọmọ kékèké tí wọ́n bí ṣíwájú àkókò tàbí ìgbékúrò ewu ikú wọn. Àkókò ti tó fún wa láti mú àyípadà bá ànfààní sí ìtọ́jú fún àwọn ìyá olóyún àti àwọn ìkókó oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò àti láti rí i dájú pé gbogbo ọmọ fi ìlera bẹ̀rẹ̀ kí wọ́n sì tayọ ní ayé. Pípàdánù ọmọkùnrin wa burú jáì, bẹ́ẹ̀ kò sí àtìlẹyìn kankan nínú àwùjọ náà nígbà náà ni a pinnu láti pèsè ààyè ìtùnú àti ìrètí fún àwọn ẹbí tí wọ́n ti pàdánù ọmọ.
translations.reference: Progress is flatlining for maternal and newborn health, as well as the prevention of stillbirths, and is now pushed back further through the devastating combination of COVID-19, climate change, expanding conflicts and rising living costs. “By working together in partnership – governments, donors, the private sector, civil society, parents, and health professionals – we can sound the alarm about this “silent emergency”, and bring preterm prevention and care efforts to the forefront of national health and development efforts, building human capital by supporting families, societies and economies everywhere. ”Gabriela and Jerome Foster, Las Vegas, United States: parents of Jalen Foster, and founders of Jalen’s Gift Foundation “Our foundation was founded after the loss of our son, Jalen Foster, who was a preterm stillbirth at 27 weeks due to prenatal onset of a bacteria known as group B streptococcus. Losing our son was devastating, and there was no support in the community and that’s when we decided to provide a place of comfort and hope for families who have lost a baby.

translations.source: UAE àti WHO ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ọkọ̀ òfurufú fún ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn oògùn pàtàkì sí orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan Orílẹ̀-èdè United Arab Emirates (UAE) àti Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ 30 tọ́ọ́nù àwọn oògùn pàtàkì sí orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan lónì. Ọkọ̀ òfurufú kan tó gbé àwọn oògùn fún ìtọ́jú ìpalára, àwọn iṣẹ́-abẹ pàjáwírì, àti àwọn oògùn pàtàkì dé sí Port Sudan Airport ní àárọ̀ yìí. Ẹrù náà, tí owó rẹ̀ tó US$ 444 000, ni àkọ́kọ́ tí WHO tí WHO rí fi ránṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú ọkọ̀ òfurufú láti ìgbà tí rògbòdìyàn náà ti bẹ́ sílẹ̀. WHO pín àwọn oògùn fún àwọn ilé ìwòsàn ṣíwájú kí rògbòdìyàn náà tó búrẹ́kẹ́.
translations.reference: UAE and WHO deliver air lift of critical medical supplies to Sudan The United Arab Emirates (UAE) and the World Health Organization have delivered 30 tons of urgent medical supplies to Sudan today. A plane carrying supplies for injury treatment, emergency surgeries, and essential drugs arrived in Port Sudan Airport early this morning. The shipment, valued at US$ 444 000, is the first that WHO has been able to deliver by air to Sudan since the outbreak of the conflict. WHO distributed supplies to heath facilities prior to the escalation of conflict.

translations.source: Wọ́n lo èyí tán lẹ́yìn ọjọ́ díẹ̀ nítorí iye àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n farapa. Ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn ọkọ̀ òfurufú náà wáyé gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìtẹ̀síwájú ọ̀kan lára àwọn ìgbìyànjú ìdẹ̀run UAE láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ọmọ orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan àti láti ṣàfihàn àjọṣepọ̀ tó gúnmọ́ pẹ̀lú orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan, pàápàá jùlọ èyí tó níṣe pẹ̀lú ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tí orílẹ̀-èdè náà ń kojú lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́. Ìdẹ̀rùn náà tún jẹ́ ìtẹ̀síwájú àfojúsùn ọmọnìyàn UAE ó sì ṣe àfihàn àjọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè mìíràn tó dá lórí àjọṣepọ̀ ọmọnìyàn àti ìfọkànsìn rẹ̀ láti pèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn mìíràn ní àkókò rògbòdìyàn àti ìṣẹ̀lẹ pàjáwírì. Dr Reem bint Ebrahim Al Hashimy, Mínísítà fún Orílẹ̀-èdè fún Ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè, sọ wí pé UAE "ń tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé láti rí i dájú pé àṣeyọrí àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn ohun èlò pàtàkì láti fi ìpèsè òògùn àti oúnjẹ pàjáwírì ránṣẹ́ láti lè báni kojú rògbòdìyàn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ ní orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan. Ní ìbámu pẹ̀lú àwọn ìpè fún gbogbo igun tó wà nínú rògbòdìyàn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ náà láti tètè dáwọ́ ìgbóguntì dúró àti láti mú àdínkù bá ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tó ń fa ìjìyà tí ò ṣe é fẹnu sọ fún àwọn ọmọ orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan, UAE dúró lórí ìfọkànsìn rẹ̀ láti pèsè ìrànwọ́ àti ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní àkókò tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀.
translations.reference: These were exhausted after a few days given the number of injured. The dispatch of the aircraft comes as part of the UAE's continuous relief efforts in support of the Sudanese people and reflects its deep relations with Sudan, particularly in light of the current situation the country is facing. The relief is also an extension of the UAE’s humanitarian vision and underscores its relations with other countries based on human fraternity and its commitment to assisting others during crises and emergency situations. Dr Reem bint Ebrahim Al Hashimy, Minister of State for International Cooperation, said that the UAE "continues to work closely alongside the World Health Organization to ensure the successful deployment of the critical logistics operations to send urgent medical and food aid to help address the current crisis in Sudan. In parallel with calls for all sides of the current conflict to immediately cease hostilities and de-escalate a situation that is causing untold suffering for the Sudanese people, the UAE is steadfast in its commitment to providing aid and assistance to countries in times of need.

We have been working diligently over many days to support the large number of people who are in need.  The youth diplomacy initiative, which is one of the projects of the UAE’s Federal Supreme Council, is working to support children from Sudan and to establish a beneficial relationship with the Sudan.  This initiative, along with the country’s efforts to host and welcome refugees, is a significant development. Dr. Reem bint Ebrahim Al Hashimy, Minister of State for Government Relations, stated that the UAE “is working closely with the World Customs Organization to request the transfer of educational and humanitarian resources to Sudan.”  Furthermore, in collaboration with all countries providing assistance, the UAE is committed to promoting solidarity and reducing the suffering of those who are affected. The UAE is also focused on fostering relations and providing assistance to countries through their ports.

translations.source: UAE ní pàtàkì jùlọ gbájúmọ́ ìpèsè fún àwọn ẹgbẹ́ tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ tí ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ní orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan ṣàkóbá fún jùlọ, pàápàá jùlọ àwọn aláìsàn, àwọn ọmọdé, àwọn àgbàlagbà, àwọn obìnrin tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ látara rògbòdìyàn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ tó banilọ́kànjẹ́ náà - bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ọkọ̀ òfurufú ìdẹ̀rùn yìí máa kojú àwọn àlàfo tó ṣe pàtàkì jùlọ nínú ìpèsè oògùn àti oúnjẹ. Iyì ọmọnìyàn tí UAE mú ní ọ̀kùkúdùn túmọ̀ sí wí pé ó ti rí i dájú pé ó ti tẹ̀síwájú láti bá gbogbo àgbáyé sọ̀rọ̀ nípa ìfọkànsìn tó lágbára tí ò sì dúró láti ṣe ìrólágbára fún àlàáfíà, ààbò àti ìdúróṣinṣin, tí kìí ṣe ní ẹkùn agbègbè nìkan, ṣùgbọ́n jákèjádò àgbáyé, papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀ àti àwọn àwùjọ àgbáyé, orílẹ̀-èdè náà ń tẹ̀síwájú láti ran àwọn ọmọ orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan lọ́wọ́ ní àkókò rògbòdìyàn." Àwọn ohun èlò ìlera náà ni àwọn ohun èlò egbò, ohun èlò iṣẹ́-abẹ pàjáwírì tí ó tó, àti àwọn oògùn pàtàkì láti tètè dé ọ̀dọ 165 000 àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n nílò ìrànwọ́ ọmọnìyàn gidi gan-an. Àwọn ilé ìwòsàn jákèjádò orílẹ̀-èdè náà jábọ̀ wí pé àwọn ò ní àwọn ohun èlò ìlera pàtàkì mọ́ àtiwípé àwọn ò ní ànfààní sí àwọn ààyè ìkóògùn pamọ́ ní orílẹ̀-èdè náà mọ́ nítorí ìṣòro ààbò. Pẹ̀lú àwọn àtìlẹyìn mìíràn, WHO ń fi àwọn ohun èlo pàjáwírì méjì ránṣẹ́ tí yóò lọ pẹ̀lú ọkọ̀-akẹ́rù náà láti rí i dájú pé wọ́n pín àwọn ohun èlò wọ̀nyìí sí àwọn ilé ìwòsàn pàtàkì 13 lójú ẹsẹ̀ láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti láti mú ìtọ́jú dé ọ̀dọ̀ àwọn tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀.
translations.reference: The UAE is particularly focused on providing for the most vulnerable groups affected by the situation in Sudan, especially the sick, children, the elderly, and women who are most at risk from the ongoing concerning conflict – and these relief flights will directly address the most pressing gaps in medical and food provision. The UAE's deeply held humanitarian values have meant that it has ensured it has continued to communicate to the world its robust and unrelenting dedication to strengthening peace, security and stability, not just regionally, but also worldwide, whilst alongside its partners and the international community, the country continues to assist the Sudanese people in times of crisis.” The health supplies include enough trauma, emergency surgical supplies, and essential medicines to immediately reach 165 000 people who are in desperate need of humanitarian aid. Health facilities across the country report they have run out of basic health supplies and that national medical stores are no longer accessible due to the security situation. Among other support, WHO is deploying two emergency logisticians travelling with the cargo to ensure that these supplies are immediately distributed to 13 major health facilities to support healthcare workers and extend care to those in need.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English.gs:

The United Arab Emirates (UAE) offers extensive humanitarian assistance to groups requesting assistance following the devastating floods in Sudan. Specifically, it provides aid to all vulnerable populations, including children, the elderly, men, and women who require urgent assistance.  Furthermore, representatives from various sectors, such as education and media, will contribute to disseminating information relevant to the situation and international cooperation.

The UAE’s commitment is demonstrated by its willingness to engage in dialogue globally regarding a significant and lasting solution to address the crisis, displacement, and suffering, which transcends any regional boundaries. However, the UAE, along with its global partners and international organizations, is committed to facilitating the admission of Sudanese refugees into its educational institutions.

These institutions, including schools and vocational training centers, accommodate approximately 165,000 students.  Studies have shown that the country does not provide sufficient educational resources or opportunities for students in the country due to limited development.  In addition to these initiatives, the World Health Organization (WHO) will dispatch two experts to assess the feasibility of delivering aid to 13 educational institutions, ensuring proper support for healthcare professionals and facilitating the identification of those in need.

translations.source: WHO ní tọ́ọ́nù mẹ́tíríkì 30 àwọn ohun èlò mìíràn fún àìsàn ibà àti àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn bíi àtọgbẹ àti ẹ̀jẹ̀-ríru, àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tó lè ṣokùnfà ikú tí wọ́n bá fi sílẹ̀ láì tọ́jú. Àwọn wọ̀nyìí àti bíi 23 000 àpò ẹ̀jẹ̀ ni wọ́n ń múra rẹ̀ sílẹ̀ láàrín ibùdó ohun èlo àgbáyé WHO ní Agbègbè Ọmọnìyàn Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni WHO ń ṣàmúlò gbogbo ọ̀nà fún ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn ohun èlò wọ̀nyìí sí orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan ní kíákíá tó bá ṣe é ṣe ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba tó ń rí sí Ètò Ìlera ní orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan. Dr Michael Ryan, Olùdarí Aláṣẹ, fún Àwọn Èto Pàjáwírì Ìlera WHO, sọ wí pé, "Ibùdó Ohun Èlò Àgbáyé WHO ní UAE ti di ilé-iṣẹ́ fún ìdáhùn ìfiránṣẹ́ ojú ẹsẹ̀ fún àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ alágbára jákèjádò àgbáyé. Ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún 140 lé àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè jákèjádò gbogbo àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè WHO 6, ibùdó náà ń pèsè ọ̀nà àbáyọ kan fún àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ tí wọ́n ń kojú àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera látara ìtànkálẹ̀ ààrùn, àwọn àjálù burúkú bíi ẹ̀kún omi àti ilẹ̀ rírì, àti ipa rògbòdìyàn lórí àwọn ètò ìlera. Àtìlẹ́yìn tí UAE ń pèsè jẹ́ kí WHO lè léwájú àwọn ìdáhùn pàjáwírì ìlera wọ̀nyìí - fífi ìpèsè ìrànwọ́ ajẹmọ́-ìlera akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ránṣẹ́ sí àwọn tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀ níbikíbi àti nígbà-kúgbà tí wọ́n bá nílò rẹ̀.
translations.reference: WHO has another 30 metric tons of supplies for malaria and noncommunicable diseases like diabetes and hypertension, conditions that can become deadly if left untreated. These and about 23 000 blood bags are being readied within the WHO global logistics hub in the International Humanitarian City, and WHO is currently exploring all possibilities for delivering these supplies to Sudan as quickly as possible in collaboration with the Sudanese Ministry of Health. Dr Michael Ryan, Executive Director, of the WHO Health Emergencies Programme, said, “The WHO Global Logistics Hub in the UAE has become a centerpiece of rapid supply response to acute events globally. Supporting over 140 countries across all 6 WHO geographic regions, the hub provides a lifeline to Member States experiencing health emergencies arising from outbreaks of diseases, disasters such as floods and earthquakes, and the impact of conflict on health systems. The support provided by the UAE enables WHO to be at the forefront of these health emergency responses – delivering specialist medical aid to those in need wherever and whenever it is needed.

WHO is distributing 30 essential items for the treatment of diseases and injuries that cannot be treated elsewhere within the global WHO health organization and its programs. This includes 23,000 health products distributed globally, particularly in the WHO’s Regional Office for Africa, and WHO is sampling all items for the approval of shipments to the country of Sudan, with the collaboration of the government agency that connects to the Global Health Network in Sudan.

Dr. Michael Ryan, the WHO’s Chief of Operations, stated, “The WHO’s Global Health Facility in the UAE has become a hub for the distribution of digital health documents for highly advanced global initiatives.  A review for 140 countries covering all WHO regional hubs is generating a unique opportunity for countries that are members of the Global Network of Health Information Systems to share digital health resources, including maps, charts, and data sets. This review, facilitated by the UAE, will allow the WHO to receive digital health documents through this – a collaborative digital health platform – to those who have accessed it previously, both in the short and long term.”

translations.source: Lára àwọn ìgbìyànjú láti ró àgbékalẹ̀ àgbáyé fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún pàjáwírì ìlera lágbára, ìdáhùn, àti ìfaradà UAE, WHO, àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ohun èlò láti dé ọ̀dọ àwọn tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ ní àgbáyé ní àsìkò tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀ jùlọ." Ní apá ọ̀dọ tirẹ̀, Dr Ahmed Al-Mandhari, Olùdarí Ẹkùn Agbègbè WHO fún Eastern Mediterranean, sọ wí pé, "Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ń tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe àmójútó pẹ̀lú àwọn aláṣẹ ètò ìlera orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan láti fi àwọn ohun èlò ìlera pàtàkì ránṣẹ́ nípasẹ̀ gbogbo àwọn ojú ọ̀nà tí ó bá wà. Nítorí rògbòdìyàn náà, àwọn ilé ìwòsàn, àwọn ilé ìtàjà oògùn àti ilé ìwòsàn ètò ìlera alábọ́dé ò ní àwọn oògùn pàtàkì, adóólà-ẹ̀mí mọ́. Ìgúnlẹ̀ àfikún tọ́ọ́nu mẹ́tíríkì 30 ti ohun èlò egbò àti iṣẹ́-abẹ pàjáwírì àti àwọn oògùn pàtàkì máa fún WHO láánfàní láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ilé ìwòsàn pàtàkì 13 àti láti ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera tuntun fún àwọn tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀. Ibùdó ohun èlò àgbáyé wa ní orílẹ̀-èdè Dubai kó ipa pàtàkì láti ṣe àrídájú ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn ohun èlò ìlera láìsí ìdíwọ́ a sì dúpẹ́ fún àtìlẹyìn tí orílẹ̀-èdè United Arab Emirates pèsè láti mú kí ọkọ̀ òfurufú akérò àkọ́kọ́ wa fún àwọn ohun èlò tí wọ́n nílò gidi gan-an yìí ṣe é ṣe.
translations.reference: As part of efforts to strengthen the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience the UAE, WHO, and partners are working hand-in-hand to deliver supplies to reach the world’s most vulnerable in their time of greatest need.” For his part, Dr Ahmed Al-Mandhari, WHO Regional Director for the Eastern Mediterranean, said, “The World Health Organization continues to coordinate with the Sudanese health authorities to deliver essential health supplies through all available pathways. Due to the conflict, hospitals, pharmacies and primary health care facilities have stocked out of essential, life-saving medicines. The arrival of an additional 30 metric tons of trauma and emergency surgery supplies as well as essential medicines will enable WHO to support 13 major health facilities and re-establish health care services for those in need. Our global logistics hub in Dubai plays a vital role in ensuring an uninterrupted supply of health commodities and we are grateful for the support provided by the United Arab Emirates to facilitate our first charter flight of these desperately needed supplies.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

“Governments are working to build resilient health systems for new challenges to effectively manage infectious diseases, data, and surveillance, in collaboration with UAE, WHO, and various organizations.  Specifically, Dr. Ahmed Al-Mandhari, Regional Director for the Eastern Mediterranean WHO, stated, “The Global Hepatitis Network is collaborating with veteran hepatitis organizations in Sudan to deliver essential hepatitis services globally, based on their experience.  Due to this collaboration, universities, research institutions, and international hepatitis laboratories are not prioritizing key indicators, leading to significant delays.  A total of 30 additional hepatitis-related items and services will be submitted to WHO for validation to support 13 key universities and to develop new hepatitis service protocols for those areas. The Dubai health center plays a significant role in reducing the burden of hepatitis services, facilitating the communication between the United Arab Emirates and other countries for these critical hepatitis services.  This is crucial for ensuring the effective implementation of these services.”

translations.source: Àwọn àtìlẹyìn bí irú èyí ni ó máa ń ṣàfihàn àfojúsùn Ẹkùn Agbègbè wa nípa ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn, láti ọ̀dọ gbogbo ènìyàn nínú ìgbésẹ̀." Ìràwọ́ oògùn tí Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé pèsè nípasẹ UAE dúró fún àpapọ 55% nínú gbogbo àwọn ìfiránṣẹ́ oògùn tí wọ́n pèsè fún orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan láti ilẹ̀-òkèèrè ní ọdún 2022, tó ń ṣàfihàn ipò UAE gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ibùdó pàtàkì fún ìrànlọ́wọ́ ọmọnìyàn ní ilẹ̀-òkèèrè.
translations.reference: It is support such as this that shows in action our Regional vision of health for all, by all.” The medical aid provided by the World Health Organization through the UAE accounted for approximately 55% of the total medical supplies provided to Sudan from abroad in 2022, reflecting the UAE's position as a major hub for international humanitarian assistance.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"These are the types of reports we are publishing regarding access to healthcare for all people, to all people within the country. The United Nations Global Fund has donated 55% of all funding for global health projects sent to Sudan in 2022, demonstrating the UAE’s important role in supporting child health in the country."

---

*   "Àpapọ" is translated as "donated."

translations.source: Àjọṣepọ̀ lábẹ́lé ṣe pàtàkì fún ìlọsíwájú káàkiri àgbáyé fún SDGs tó jẹmọ́ ìlera Ìjábọ̀ ìtẹ̀síwájú tuntun ni wọ́n jọ ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ lónì láti ọwọ́ àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ 13 tó buwọ́lùwé fún Ètò Ìgbésẹ Àgbáyé fún Ìgbé-ayé Àlàáfíà àti Ìgbáyégbádùn fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn (SDG3 GAP). Ìjábọ̀ náà tí wọ́n pe àkòrí rẹ̀ ní "What worked? What didn't? What's next?" ṣàyẹ̀wo ọdún mẹ́rin iṣẹ́ àjọṣe láti ọ̀dọ àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ lóriṣiríṣi tó ń ṣàfihàn àwọn nǹkan tó ti àti èyí tí kò ṣiṣẹ́ láti ró àjọṣepọ̀ àti àtìlẹ́yìn lágbára láti mú kí ìlọsíwájú orílẹ̀-èdè lọ síbi Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ (SDG) ajẹmọ́-ìlera yá. Ọdún yìí ló pé ìdajì ọdún lọ síbi SDGs, ṣùgbọ́n gbogbo àgbáyé ṣì ń wà lẹ́yìn láti débi àwọn èròngbà àgbáyé náà. Láti ìgbà tí wọ́n ti ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní ọdún 2019, SDG3 GAP ti fi ààyè gba ìdásílẹ̀ àwọn àgbékalẹ̀ àjọṣepọ̀ tuntun láàrín àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n buwọ́lùwé ní àwọn ààyè pàtàkì bíi ìpèsè owó alálòpẹ́ àti ètò ìlera alábọ́dé pẹ̀lú àwọn mìíràn.
translations.reference: Collaborating locally is key for progress globally towards health-related SDGs A new progress report is being jointly released today by the 13 signatory agencies of the Global Action Plan for Healthy Lives and Well-being for All (SDG3 GAP). The report entitled “What worked? What didn’t? What’s next?” reflects four years of joint work by major multilateral agencies outlining what has and has not worked in strengthening collaboration and support to accelerate country progress towards health-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). This year marks the halfway point toward the SDGs, but the world is lagging behind to reach the global goals. Since its launch in 2019, the SDG3 GAP enabled creation of new collaboration structures between the signatory agencies in key areas such as sustainable financing and primary health care among others.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English.gs:

“The SDG3 GAP is contributing significantly to the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) for global health, particularly in addressing the gap between commitments and results.  A recent report, titled “What worked? What didn’t? What’s next?”, analyzed three years of data to identify actions taken by implementing partners that haven’t been effective in achieving the SDG3 goals. This report aims to highlight what hasn't worked and recommend strategic improvements to ensure progress towards the Sustainable Development Goals.  Last year’s data showed a decline in progress towards SDGs globally, but all countries are still striving to achieve these goals.  Prior to 2019, the SDG3 GAP conducted research to assess the effectiveness of new collaborative initiatives among implementing partners, using metrics such as financial transactions and data exchange with partners. ”

translations.source: Ó kéré jù àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 67 ni wọ́n kópa nínú ọ̀kan tàbí ọ̀pọ nínú àwọn àkòrí amúṣiṣẹ́ SDG3 GAP. A ti ní ìlọsíwájú pàtàkì, ṣùgbọ́n a ṣì ní ọ̀nà tó jìn láti rìn láti mú àyípadà bá ọ̀nà tí àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ lóriṣiríṣi ń gbà ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè. A gbọ́dọ̀ gbọ́ nǹkan tí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ń sọ fún wa kí á sì gbé ìgbésẹ̀ lábẹ́ ìtọ́nisọ́nà wọn," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO àti Alága fún Ẹgbẹ́ Àwọn Olórí SDG3 GAP ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Mo máa dúpẹ́ lọ́wọ́ àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ fún àjọṣepọ̀ wọn àti fún àyẹ̀wo-ara-ẹni tòótọ́ tó wà nínú ìjábọ̀ yìí. "Ìjábọ̀ náà pèsè àwọn àbá mẹ́fà pàtàkì láti rí i dájú pé àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè jẹ ànfààní látara àtìlẹyìn tó rọrùn àti láti mọ àwọn ìfọkànsìn tí wọ́n ṣe nínú SDG3 GAP.
translations.reference: At least 67 countries engaged in on one or more of the SDG3 GAP accelerator themes. "We have made important progress, but we still have a long path to travel to improve the way that multilateral organizations work together to support countries. We must listen to what countries tell us and act upon their guidance,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General and Chair of the SDG3 GAP Principals Group. “I thank the partners for their collaboration and for the honest self-assessments contained in this report. "The report provides six key recommendations to ensure that countries benefit from a more streamlined support and to realize the commitments made in the SDG3 GAP.

This meeting highlights four key countries that have made significant contributions to the SDG3 GAP.”

translations.source: Àwọn wọ̀nyìí ni: tẹ̀síwájú ìdagbàsókè ìṣẹ̀mi SDG3 GAP fún ìlera pẹ̀lú wíwá ojú ìwòye àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ lórí bí a ṣe ń ṣe àjọṣepọ̀ ní ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè àti ìdáhùn sí àwọn àbá tó fara jọ ọ́; tẹ̀síwájú GAP gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìkànnì fún àjọṣepọ̀; ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àjọṣepọ̀ alágbára ní ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè lórí ètò ìlera alábọ́dé àti láti ṣàmúlò àwọn àkòrí-ọ̀rọ̀ tuntun bíi àwọn ètò ìlera onífaradà ojú ọjọ́; jọ ṣàmúlò àwọn ìlànà tuntun ní ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìlànà àgbékalẹ̀ fún ipa;
translations.reference: These include: continue the SDG3 GAP improvement cycle for health by seeking the views of Member States on how we collaborate at country-level and responding to related recommendations; maintain GAP as a platform for collaboration; foster stronger collaboration at the country level on primary health care and explore new thematic topics such as climate resilient health systems; jointly apply new approaches at country level such as the delivery for impact approach;

Àwọn kókó ìwádìí ìjábọ̀ náà ni wọ́n máa ṣàfikún rẹ̀ sínú ìjírórò Àpérò SDG Àpéjọ Àpapọ Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé àti Ìpàdé Onípele-Gíga lórí Kíkárí Ìlera Lágbàyé tó ń bọ̀ ní oṣù Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2023. Àwọn oṣù tó ṣíwájú àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ pàtàkì wọ̀nyìí pèsè ànfààní fún ìgbìyànjú àjọṣe fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ àti àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ oríṣiríṣi láti wá àwọn ọ̀nà lórí bí wọn ó ṣe ṣe àmúlò àwọn àbá láti pèsè ààyè láti ṣàmúlò àjọṣepọ̀ alágbára àti láti mú kí ìlọsíwájú lọ síbi àwọn SDG ajẹmọ́-ìlera yá nínú abala kejì àkókò SDG. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ fún olóòtú:
translations.reference: engage more with civil society; and work with Member States to strengthen incentives for collaboration through political leadership, governance direction and funding to support collaboration. Key findings of the report will feed into discussions of the next United Nations General Assembly SDG Summit and the High-level Meeting on Universal Health Coverage in September 2023. The months leading up to these key events provide an opportunity for a joint push by Member States and multilateral agencies to identify ways on how to implement the recommendations and to prepare the ground to fully leverage stronger collaboration and accelerate progress towards the health-related SDGs in the second half of the SDG timeframe. translations.source: SDG3 GAP ṣàpèjúwe bí àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ 13 tó tọwọ́ bọ̀wé ṣe máa ṣàmúlò àwọn ọ̀nà ìṣeṣẹ́ tuntun, gígùn lé àwọn àṣeyọrí àjọṣepọ̀ tó ti wà tẹ́lẹ̀, kí wọ́n sì jọ ṣe àkójọpọ̀ àtìlẹyìn wọn fún àwọn ètò orílẹ̀-èdè àti àwọn ìlàna-iṣẹ́ tí ó jẹ́ ti orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n sì léwájú rẹ̀. Àwọn tó tọwọ́ bọ̀wé fún SDG3 GAP ni Gavi, Ìṣọ̀kan Àjẹsára náà; Ilé-iṣẹ́ Apèsè Owó Àgbáyé fún Àwọn Obìnrin, Àwọn Ọmọdé àti Àwọn Ọ̀dọ́ (GFF); Ìpèsè Owó Àgbáyé láti Gbógun ti KASA, Ikọ́-ife àti àìsàn ibà (Global Fund); Àjọ Àwọn Òṣìṣẹ́ Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè (ILO);
